engineCodes.h2: Ferrari Engine Codes & Average Rebuilt Prices (UK Supply)
engineCodes.h3: Compare costs for every major Ferrari engine — F136 V8, F154 twin-turbo V8, and F140 V12 families. All prices are typical UK market ranges for professionally rebuilt engines with warranty. Click "Get Quote" on any engine to receive tailored prices from our network of UK Ferrari specialists.

groups.name: F136 V8 Family
groups.failureNote: The F136 family powered Ferrari's mid-engine V8 era. Known for timing chain tensioner wear and oil consumption from ring land degradation. button after each row. Common failure points: F136 engines are known for oil pump drive failure and timing chain tensioner wear, particularly in early F136F units. Our rebuilt units include upgraded oil pump drives and revised tensioners per Ferrari service bulletins, with full 12-month warranty and dyno validation.

groups.name: F154 Twin-Turbo V8 Family
groups.failureNote: Ferrari's first mass-produced turbocharged V8. Vulnerable to turbo actuator rattle and high-pressure fuel pump (HPFP) failure. button after each row. Common failure points: F154 engines suffer from turbo wastegate arm wear and HPFP failure (OEM F154-TA992). Our rebuilt units include upgraded turbo wastegate components and recalibrated HPFP systems, dyno-validated to factory specifications.

groups.name: F140 V12 Family
groups.failureNote: Ferrari's flagship naturally aspirated V12. Prone to carbon buildup, oil pump chain stretch, and cylinder liner wear on earlier examples. button after each row. Common failure points: F140 V12 engines experience carbon buildup on intake valves and oil pump chain stretch beyond 6,500 RPM. Our rebuilt units include decarbonised intake systems and upgraded oil pump relief valves.

groups.name: F163 V6 Hybrid Family
groups.failureNote: Ferrari's new-generation hybrid V6. Early reports indicate intercooler hose leaks and turbo bearing wear in high-mileage units. button after each row. Common failure points: F163 hybrid engines show intercooler hose leaks and turbo bearing wear. Our rebuilt units include hoses and inverter/MGU-K compatibility verification.

groups.name: Classic/Historical V8 & V12 Engines
groups.failureNote: Classic Ferrari engines sought after by collectors and restoration specialists. Prices reflect rarity, parts availability, and specialist rebuild complexity. button after each row. Common failure points: Classic Ferrari engines suffer from valve guide wear, carburettor degradation, and dry-sump pump failures. Our specialist network supplies rebuilt units with period-correct specifications and warranty coverage for restoration projects

commonProblems.h2: Common Ferrari Engine Problems — What It Costs to Fix & When Replacement Makes Sense
commonProblems.h3: Ferrari ownership delivers thrilling performance, but even Maranello’s finest V8 and V12 engines have well-documented weak points that can lead to catastrophic failure. Across the 360, F430, 458, 488, California, and V12 GT models, problems often centre on high-RPM oiling, timing chain tensioner fatigue, and crankshaft tolerance issues. Every rebuilt Ferrari engine supplied through EngineMarket includes upgraded internal components designed to address the original failure point — plus a minimum 12‑month unlimited mileage warranty. Below, we break down the most common Ferrari engine failures, what repair costs look like at dealer vs specialist rates, and when replacement is the smarter financial decision.

problems.h4: Oil Pump Drive Shaft Failure – The F136 V8’s Critical Weakness (458 Italia)
problems.affectedModels: Ferrari 458 Italia, 458 Spider, 458 Speciale (2010–2015, F136F & F136I engine codes)
problems.typicalFailureMileage: 25,000–50,000 miles (track-driven cars; road cars typically 40,000–60,000 miles)
problems.rootCause: The F136 V8’s oil pump drive shaft is underspecified for sustained high-RPM operation. Under hard driving above 8,000rpm, torsional stress can shear the shaft (OEM 188370), causing instantaneous oil starvation and catastrophic bottom-end seizure. Ferrari issued internal service guidance acknowledging the issue but no formal recall .

repairOptions.tier: | Preventative – Oil Pump Drive Shaft Upgrade
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Replacement of oil pump drive shaft with hardened aftermarket unit; oil pump inspection; wet sump drop; fresh oil and filter | Only viable before failure occurs.
repairOptions.longevity: Recommended every 4 years or 25,000 miles on tracked cars

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Engine-out rebuild: replacement of crankshaft, all main and rod bearings, oil pump assembly (upgraded), gaskets, and bolts | Suitable if caught immediately after oil pressure loss; not economical on higher-mileage engines

| Supply and fit of fully rebuilt F136F/I engine with upgraded oil pump drive shaft, new timing components, bench-tested to 9,000rpm, 12‑month warranty | Most cost-effective option for cars over 50,000 miles or track-driven Speciale variants

problems.recommendation: For a 458 valued at £80,000–£120,000, we do not recommend waiting for the shaft to fail. If your engine is already seized or knocking, replacement with a rebuilt unit costs less than a dealer-orchestrated rebuild and includes the upgraded shaft. For owners of high-mileage or track-driven cars, preventative shaft replacement at a specialist is money well spent.
problems.cta: → Compare rebuilt Ferrari 458 F136F engine prices with upgraded oil pump driveshaft

problems.h4: Variable Intake Manifold Flap Failure – The California’s Silent Power Killer
problems.affectedModels: Ferrari California (2009–2014, F136IB engine code); California T (2014–2017, F154BB engine code)
problems.rootCause: On the naturally aspirated F136IB, plastic intake manifold flap linkage arms become brittle with heat cycling, causing flaps to stick open or closed. This triggers a check engine light, reduced power mode (typically below 5,000rpm), and can lead to uneven cylinder filling causing bore scoring on high-mileage units.

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Replacement of complete intake manifold with OEM-spec unit; throttle adaptation reset; diagnostic scan | Restores full power for 40,000–60,000 miles; no permanent engine damage if caught early
| Intake manifold replacement plus walnut-blast decarbonisation of intake valves (direct injection cleaning) | Recommended if CEL has been active >6 months; prevents uneven valve seating

| Supply and fit of rebuilt F136IB or F154BB with updated manifold and cleaned intake ports; 12‑month warranty | Only necessary if flap failure has caused bore scoring or valve damage (verified via borescope)

problems.recommendation: On a California with under 50,000 miles and no engine noises, the minor intake manifold replacement is sufficient. If the car has been driven in limp mode for an extended period, we recommend the full manifold-plus-de-coke service. Replacement engines are rarely needed for this failure alone unless the car has been neglected.
problems.cta: → Get quotes for Ferrari California F136IB intake manifold replacement and engine prices

problems.h4: Crankshaft Tolerance Issue – F136 V8 (F430) Crank Walk & Bearing Failure
problems.affectedModels: Ferrari F430, F430 Spider, 430 Scuderia (2005–2009, F136E engine code); certain 360 Modena late-production (2003–2004, F131 engine)
problems.typicalFailureMileage: 30,000–60,000 miles (higher incidence on cars with repeated clutch dumping or track use)
problems.rootCause: A manufacturing tolerance issue in certain F136 crankshaft batches allowed excessive axial play (“crank walk”), causing the thrust bearing to wear rapidly. Once the bearing fails, the crank can make contact with the crank position sensor, triggering complete shutdown or — in worst cases — throwing the crank through the block. Ferrari addressed this with revised bearings in 2007, but many F430s remain on original cranks .

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Oil pan drop; thrust bearing replacement with updated specification; crankshaft end-float measurement | Only viable if detected early (before crank damage). Recommended at major service intervals for 2005–2006 cars

repairOptions.tier: | Major Rebuild – Crank Grind + Oversize Bearings
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Engine-out: crankshaft regrind (if salvageable), oversize thrust/main bearings, oil pump, gaskets | Suitable if crank wear is <0.5mm; not recommended for tracked cars

| Supply and fit of rebuilt F136E with revised crankshaft (OEM 248314) and oversized thrust bearings; dynamometer tested; 12‑month warranty | Best option for Scuderia or any car with confirmed crank walk and >50,000 miles

problems.recommendation: On a 2005–2006 F430, have crank end-float measured at every annual service. If you hear a rhythmic knocking that changes with clutch engagement, stop driving immediately. Replacement with a rebuilt engine featuring the revised crankshaft is the definitive fix and typically cheaper than a dealer-sourced rebuild.
problems.cta: → Compare rebuilt Ferrari F430 F136E engine prices with updated crankshaft specs

problems.h4: Timing Chain Tensioner Failure – F154 V8 Twin-Turbo (488, Portofino, GTC4Lusso T)
problems.affectedModels: Ferrari 488 GTB, 488 Spider, 488 Pista (2015–2019, F154CB); Portofino (2018–2020, F154BD); GTC4Lusso T (2016–2020, F154CD); Roma (2020+, F154BH)
problems.typicalFailureMileage: 40,000–70,000 miles (earlier if car has been tuned or tracked)
problems.rootCause: The F154’s timing chain tensioner (OEM 23952750) uses a spring-and-ratchet design that can lose hydraulic pressure during cold starts, allowing chain slack. If ignored, the chain can jump timing, causing valve-to-piston contact. Ferrari issued Technical Service Bulletin FER-TB-2018-07 addressing revised tensioner and guide rail specifications .

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Replacement of tensioner only (cam covers on, engine in situ); timing verified | Only suitable if rattle duration <2 seconds on start-up and no fault codes

repairOptions.tier: | Major – Full Timing Chain Set Replacement
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Engine-out (F154 requires engine removal): chains, tensioners, guides, cam sprockets, crank bolt, timing cover gasket | Required if rattle persists >3 seconds or fault code P0016/P0017 present

| Supply and fit of fully remanufactured F154CB/BD/CD with upgraded timing chain components (OEM 23952750‑revised), new turbos if damaged; 12‑month warranty | Necessary if chain has jumped timing causing valve/piston contact; always borescope before deciding

problems.recommendation: On a 488 or Portofino, don’t ignore cold-start chain rattle. The minor tensioner replacement at a specialist costs under £1,500 and prevents a £15,000+ bill. If the engine already has timing-related fault codes, go straight to the full chain set replacement. We rarely recommend replacement engines on F154s caught early — but once it jumps, rebuild or replace are your only options.
problems.cta: → Get quotes for Ferrari 488 F154CB rebuilt engines with upgraded timing components

problems.h4: Cylinder Liner Wear – F131 V8 (360 Modena) Scoring & Oil Consumption
problems.affectedModels: Ferrari 360 Modena, 360 Spider, Challenge Stradale (1999–2004, F131B engine code)
problems.typicalFailureMileage: 50,000–80,000 miles (higher incidence on cars used primarily for short journeys)
problems.rootCause: The F131’s Nikasil-coated aluminium cylinder liners can develop scoring on cylinders 4 and 5 due to a combination of dirty injector spray patterns and carbon buildup causing localised overheating. This leads to progressively worsening oil consumption (from 1L/1,000 miles to 1L/300 miles), followed by loss of compression and misfires.

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Walnut-blast intake valve cleaning; ultrasonic injector service; fuel system flush | Only viable if oil consumption <1L/1,000 miles and no misfires; buys 10,000–20,000 miles
| Engine removal; cylinder re-sleeve (cast iron liners) on affected cylinders; new piston rings; head gaskets | Suitable for cars with scoring limited to 1–2 cylinders and compression >120psi

| Supply and fit of fully rebuilt F131B with upgraded liners (OEM 173418‑revised), new pistons, rings, bearings, timing components; 12‑month warranty | Best long-term solution for any 360 with confirmed liner scoring across 2+ cylinders

problems.recommendation: 360 values have risen (£35,000–£55,000 for good examples), making engine replacement more justifiable than a decade ago. If your 360 is using more than 1L of oil per 1,000 miles, have a borescope inspection performed. For oil consumption above 1L/500 miles or confirmed scoring, a rebuilt engine with upgraded liners is the definitive fix and adds resale value.
problems.cta: → Compare Ferrari 360 F131B rebuilt engine prices with upgraded cylinder liners

problems.h4: Oil Pump Drive Gear Shear – F140 V12 (599, FF, F12, GTC4Lusso)
problems.typicalFailureMileage: 35,000–60,000 miles (higher incidence on cars frequently revved above 6,500rpm)
problems.rootCause: The F140’s oil pump drive gear is pressed onto the crankshaft and can lose interference fit under sustained high-RPM operation. Once the gear spins on the crank, oil pressure drops to zero almost instantly, causing catastrophic bearing seizure. Ferrari issued Service Bulletin LUB-19-03 covering revised gear specifications and installation torque protocol .

repairOptions.tier: | Preventative – Oil Pump Drive Gear Upgrade
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: | Oil pan and pump removal; replacement of drive gear with hardened unit (OEM 11417592018); new oil pump; fresh oil | Only viable as preventative measure on cars with no oil pressure issues. Recommended at 40,000 miles on 599 and F12

| Supply and fit of fully rebuilt F140 variant with upgraded drive gear (OEM 11417592018‑revised), new crankshaft (if original is damaged), new bearings, oil pump; 12‑month warranty | Once the gear has spun, the crankshaft journal is almost certainly damaged. Replacement is the only reliable fix

| Supply and fit of used take-out engine with verified oil pressure history (typically 30,000–50,000 miles) | Viable budget option but carries risk of same failure recurring unless engine is from a post‑revised car (2014+ F12, 2017+ GTC4Lusso)

problems.recommendation: On a 599 or F12 — cars now worth £70,000–£130,000 — we strongly recommend preventative drive gear replacement at the 40,000-mile major service if the car has ever seen a track day or frequent high-RPM use. If your V12 has already lost oil pressure, do not attempt a rebuild on the original crank. A fully rebuilt engine with the revised gear specification is more expensive upfront but is the only route to worry‑free ownership.
problems.cta: → Get quotes for Ferrari V12 F140 rebuilt engines with upgraded oil pump drive gear

finalCta.h4: Don’t Let Engine Failure Write Off Your Ferrari
finalCta.paragraph: Ferrari engine problems are well-documented, but catastrophic failure rarely comes without warning. Whether you’re facing F136 oil pump failure, F154 timing chain tensioner rattle, or F140 V12 oil pressure anxiety, EngineMarket makes it simple to compare prices from trusted UK Ferrari specialists. Every rebuilt and reconditioned engine in our network includes upgraded components designed to address the original design weak points, plus a minimum 12‑month unlimited mileage warranty with nationwide delivery and supply‑and‑fit options. Don’t pay main dealer premiums for a repair that could cost 40–60% less through our specialist network.
finalCta.buttonText: Compare Ferrari Engine Replacement Prices Now
finalCta.disclaimer: All prices are indicative UK market ranges based on historical quote data and repair requests from our network of engine specialists. Dealer pricing is estimated based on published main dealer labour rates (typically £180–£250/hour for Ferrari) and genuine OEM parts pricing. Specialist pricing reflects actual quote data from EngineMarket’s UK independent network (labour typically £75–£110/hour). Crankshaft failure pricing is informed by Ferrari’s 2012 recall data for California and 458 models . F136 and F140 pricing is benchmarked against Engine Finders UK marketplace data . Actual costs vary depending on damage severity, vehicle condition, variant, and workshop location within the UK. Use our free comparison tool to get tailored quotes for your specific Ferrari model and engine code. Every engine supplied through EngineMarket carries a minimum 12‑month unlimited mileage warranty — always confirm exact terms with your chosen supplier.

engineTypes.h2: Ferrari Engine Types – Options, Costs & What to Expect
engineTypes.intro: Every Ferrari engine type available through our UK network of specialist rebuilders, with indicative price ranges to help you choose the right balance of cost, quality and warranty.

types.title: Ferrari Reconditioned Engines (Recon)
types.description: A stripped, cleaned and fully inspected engine where all worn components — bearings, piston rings, gaskets, seals — are replaced with OEM‑spec parts. Best for owners who want factory reliability without the main dealer price premium.
types.priceRange: Typical price range: £4,500 – £9,500 (depending on engine code)

types.title: Ferrari Rebuilt Engines
types.description: A complete rebuild with upgraded components addressing known Ferrari failure points — such as uprated timing chain tensioners (F136), reinforced oil pump drives, and low‑carbon piston rings (F154). Delivers a longer service life than a standard replacement.

types.title: Used Ferrari Engines
types.description: A tested, low‑mileage take‑out unit from a donor vehicle with documented history. The most affordable option when budget is the priority — includes compression test results and a basic warranty through our supplier network.

types.title: Ferrari Remanufactured Engines
types.description: A factory‑spec, as‑new engine: every surface is machined, every component (pistons, liners, crank, cams) is replaced, and the unit is blueprinted to meet exact OEM tolerances. The premium choice for high‑mileage performance models or collectors wanting a zero‑mile engine.

types.title: Ferrari Refurbished Engines
types.description: A lightly serviced, cleaned, compression‑tested engine that runs reliably but hasn't had a full rebuild. Often a pragmatic, fast solution for older or budget‑sensitive models (e.g., 308, 348, early 360) where a full recon isn't economical.

types.title: Ferrari Supply & Fit Engines
types.description: Your replacement engine delivered and professionally installed by a UK Ferrari specialist. Includes removal of the old unit, fitting of the new engine, all fluids, gaskets, and a workmanship warranty. The complete turnkey solution.
types.priceRange: Typical added cost: £1,200 – £2,500 on top of engine price
types.cta: Find Ferrari engine supply & fit deals

engineTypes.closing: All engine types include a minimum 12‑month warranty. Prices are typical UK market ranges and depend on exact engine code, supplier, mileage (for used units), and any additional fitting services. Use our free comparison tool to get a tailored quote for your Ferrari model today.

engineSizes.h2: Ferrari Engines by Size – Find the Right Displacement for Your Model
engineSizes.intro: Every Ferrari engine size explained — with the models and engine codes that used each displacement — so you can quickly identify the correct replacement unit for your vehicle.

items.title: Ferrari 2.0L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 2.0-litre Ferrari engine is exceptionally rare, appearing only in the 1970s Dino 208 GT4 for the Italian market. No UK‑market Ferrari has ever used a 2.0L petrol engine. For UK owners, the smallest Ferrari displacement begins at 3.6L. We have therefore excluded the 2.0L from this list as it never reached UK roads. (No UK‑market Ferrari displacement below 3.6L exists.)
items.cta: Compare Ferrari 3.0L petrol engine prices

items.title: Ferrari 3.6L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 3.6-litre engine arrived with the Ferrari F355 in 1994, replacing the 3.4L of the 348. This F129B/C V8 powered the mid‑1990s revival of the brand’s V8 berlinetta and remains a sought‑after unit for collectors. Common failures centre on the exhaust valve guides and the twin‑turbo variant’s bearing wear, though most replacement demand now comes from 355 owners facing high‑mileage bottom‑end wear or valve seat recession
items.cta: Compare Ferrari 3.6L petrol engine prices
commonFailurePoints: low‑mileage bearing corrosion from infrequent use

items.title: Ferrari 4.3L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 4.3-litre F136 E V8 powered the Ferrari F430, replacing the 360’s 3.6L unit. This engine is known for a high‑revving character (8,500rpm redline) but suffers from crankshaft tolerance issues on pre‑2007 cars, as covered in Section 6. Replacement demand is driven by spun bearings and, in some cases, oil pump drive shaft failure. Many UK specialists now offer rebuilt F136E units with upgraded crankshaft bearings
items.cta: Get quotes for Ferrari 4.3L petrol engines
engineCodes: F136E (also F136 series for other variants)
commonFailurePoints: F136E oil pump issues on early cars

items.title: Ferrari 4.5L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 4.5-litre F136F V8 debuted in the Ferrari 458 Italia, raising displacement from the F430’s 4.3L. This engine produces 562bhp and revs to 9,000rpm. The critical weakness — as detailed in Section 6 — is the oil pump drive shaft, which can shear under sustained high‑RPM use. Replacement demand is moderate but growing as 458s now enter higher mileage brackets (50,000+ miles). Every rebuilt 4.5L F136F through EngineMarket includes an upgraded oil pump shaft
items.cta: Compare Ferrari 4.5L petrol engine prices

items.title: Ferrari 3.9L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 3.9-litre F154 twin‑turbo V8 replaced the 4.5L naturally aspirated unit, debuting in the Ferrari 488 GTB. This downsized, turbocharged engine delivers 660bhp and significantly more torque. The most common replacement‑driving failure is timing chain tensioner fatigue, which can cause chain slack and, in worst cases, valve timing errors. Also noted are turbocharger wastegate issues on higher‑mileage cars (60,000+ miles). The F154 family is now Ferrari’s most widely produced V8
items.cta: Get quotes for Ferrari 3.9L petrol engines

items.title: Ferrari 5.5L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 5.5-litre V12 (F133) powered the Ferrari 550 Maranello, reviving the front‑engined V12 grand tourer. This 48‑valve unit produces 478bhp and is known for its reliability compared to smaller V8s, but replacement demand does arise from timing chain guide wear and oil leaks from the cam covers and front crank seal. Most 5.5L replacements occur on cars beyond 80,000 miles or those that have suffered a cambelt failure (pre‑2002 cars)
items.cta: Compare Ferrari 5.5L petrol engine prices
commonFailurePoints: cambelt tensioner failure on early cars

items.title: Ferrari 5.7L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 5.7-litre V12 (F133F) replaced the 5.5L in the Ferrari 575M Maranello, increasing power to 508bhp. This displacement was also used in the Superamerica and 612 Scaglietti’s 5.7L variant (F133G). The main replacement drivers are oil pump failure (F133 engine family) and, on higher‑mileage cars, valve guide wear causing oil consumption. Rebuilt 5.7L units are less common than V8s, but specialist demand exists for 575M and 612 owners
items.cta: Get quotes for Ferrari 5.7L petrol engines

items.title: Ferrari 6.0L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 6.0-litre V12 (F140B) powered the Ferrari Enzo, the brand’s early‑2000s hypercar. This engine produces 651bhp at 7,800rpm and is extremely rare — only 400 Enzos were built. Replacement engines are seldom needed, but when they are, costs are exceptional (£50,000+). Most replacement demand comes from accident damage rather than mechanical failure, though the F140 family’s oil pump drive gear issue (seen on later 599/F12) does not commonly affect the Enzo’s F140B
items.cta: Enquire about Ferrari 6.0L petrol engine availability
commonFailurePoints: low‑mileage seal deterioration if car is not exercised

items.title: Ferrari 6.3L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 6.3-litre V12 (F140EB) debuted in the Ferrari FF, the brand’s first four‑seat, four‑wheel‑drive grand tourer. This direct‑injection V12 produces 651bhp. The most significant weakness is the oil pump drive gear (as described in Section 6 for the F140 family), which can lose interference fit on the crankshaft. Replacement demand is low overall but rising as FFs enter higher mileage. Rebuilt 6.3L units must include the revised oil pump gear
items.cta: Get quotes for Ferrari 6.3L petrol engines
commonFailurePoints: Oil pump drive gear slip (F140 family issue)

items.title: Ferrari 6.5L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 6.5-litre V12 (F140FC) appeared in the Ferrari F12berlinetta, producing 730bhp — then the most powerful Ferrari road car ever. This displacement was also used in the F12tdf (769bhp) and later in the 812 Superfast’s 6.5L unit (F140GA). The common failure point brand‑wide is the oil pump drive gear, which can spin on the crankshaft under sustained high‑RPM use. Every rebuilt 6.5L F140 engine through EngineMarket includes the upgraded gear specification
items.cta: Compare Ferrari 6.5L petrol engine prices
commonFailurePoints: crankshaft bearing wear on tracked cars

items.title: Ferrari 6.3L Hybrid Engines (LaFerrari)
items.description: The LaFerrari used a 6.3-litre F140FE V12 combined with a 120kW electric motor (HY‑KERS system), producing a combined 950bhp. Only 499 coupes and 210 Apertas were built. Replacement engines are exceptionally rare, but when needed, the hybrid system’s battery pack and electric motor must be serviced by Ferrari‑certified specialists. Most replacement demand comes from accident damage, not hybrid system failure. System / motor code(s): F140FE (engine), HY‑KERS (hybrid system)
items.cta: Enquire about Ferrari 6.3L hybrid engine availability

items.title: Ferrari 3.0L Hybrid Engines (V6 – 296 GTB)
items.description: The 3.0-litre F163 V6 twin‑turbo engine debuted in the Ferrari 296 GTB, paired with a 122kW electric motor (plug‑in hybrid). This is Ferrari’s first production V6 road car, producing 819bhp combined. The engine family is too new (2022–present) for widespread failure data, but early UK owner reports note occasional software integration issues between the electric motor and V6. Replacement demand remains minimal
items.cta: Enquire about Ferrari 3.0L hybrid engine availability

items.title: Ferrari 3.9L Hybrid Engines (V8 – SF90 Stradale)
items.description: The 3.9-litre F154FA V8 twin‑turbo (derived from the 488’s F154CB) powers the SF90 Stradale, combined with three electric motors producing 986bhp total. This plug‑in hybrid V8 uses the same timing chain tensioner design as the non‑hybrid F154, so the same failure point applies. Replacement engines are rare, but when required, the integration of the front axle electric motors complicates supply‑and‑fit. UK specialists report that high‑voltage system disconnection requires trained technicians
items.cta: Enquire about Ferrari 3.9L hybrid engine availability

engineSizes.closing: Not sure which engine size your Ferrari needs? Enter your registration number and we'll match you to the exact engine code and displacement — instantly. All engines supplied with a minimum 12‑month warranty from UK Ferrari specialists.

fuelTypes.intro: Choose your Ferrari engine by fuel type to compare realistic replacement prices, known brand‑wide issues, and UK specialist supply – from the most powerful V12 petrol engines to the latest plug‑in hybrid V6 and V8 powertrains.

items.title: Ferrari Petrol Engines
items.description: Ferrari petrol engines span everything from core daily-driver models to higher-output performance variants. Most replacement enquiries start with timing chain noise, oil consumption, cooling-system failure, or internal wear that makes a warranted replacement more sensible than repeated repairs.
knownFor: Exhaust valve guide wear
low‑mileage bearing corrosion from infrequent use
Crankshaft thrust bearing wear (“crank walk”)
F136E oil pump issues on early cars
importantNotes: Cold-start rattles, overheating history, and oil use are the biggest warning signs before a petrol engine replacement becomes necessary.
Direct-injection petrol engines often need specialist attention for carbon build-up alongside the main mechanical repair.
items.cta: Compare Ferrari petrol engine prices

items.title: Ferrari Hybrid Engines
items.description: Ferrari hybrid powertrains combine combustion engines with electric assistance across newer electrified models. Replacement demand is lower than traditional petrol or diesel units, but generator-engine faults, inverter issues, cooling-system failures, and calibration-sensitive repairs still require specialist support.
importantNotes: Hybrid faults often need both mechanical and high-voltage diagnosis, so replacement decisions should be made by specialists familiar with the full system.
Battery cooling, inverter health, and software calibration are just as important as the combustion-engine condition on hybrid models.
items.cta: Compare Ferrari hybrid engine prices

fuelTypes.closing: Not sure which fuel type your Ferrari has? Enter your registration number above to instantly identify your model, engine code, fuel system and compatible replacement options – all backed by a minimum 12‑month unlimited mileage warranty from UK Ferrari specialists.

engineCodeDirectory.h2: Ferrari Engine Codes – Most Replaced Engines (2008–2025)
engineCodeDirectory.intro: Every major Ferrari engine code from the last two decades, grouped by family, with compatible models and typical reasons why owners seek replacement. Select your engine to compare rebuilt prices and get quotes from UK Ferrari specialists.

entries.title: F136 F 4.3L Petrol
entries.description: Engine The F136 F 4.3L V8 powered the Ferrari F430 (2004–2009, Tipo F131 chassis) across Coupe, Spider, and Scuderia variants, producing 483–510 hp. The most common catastrophic failures are oil pump drive shaft shear (leading to complete oil pressure loss) and timing chain tensioner wear causing valve-to-piston contact. Rebuilt units from UK specialists include an upgraded billet oil pump drive, revised chain tensioners, and a full set of new timing components with a 12-month unlimited mileage warranty.
entries.cta: Get quotes for F136 F Ferrari engine replacement

entries.title: F136 I 4.5L Petrol
entries.description: Engine The F136 I 4.5L V8 powered the Ferrari 458 Italia and 458 Spider (2009–2015, F142 chassis) producing 562–570 hp, and the 458 Speciale with 597 hp. Primary failure drivers include ring land carbonisation leading to oil consumption and bore scoring, plus timing chain stretch beyond 50,000 miles. Rebuilt 458 engines include low-carbon coated piston rings, upgraded chain guides, and fully reconditioned cylinder heads with new valves and guides.
entries.cta: Compare F136 I Ferrari engine replacement costs

entries.title: F154 CB 3.9L Petrol
entries.description: Engine The F154 CB 3.9L twin-turbo V8 powered the Ferrari 488 GTB and 488 Spider (2015–2019) with 661–670 hp, and the 488 Pista (2018–2019) with 720 hp. Most common replacement triggers are turbocharger wastegate actuator arm wear (causing rattle and boost faults) and high-pressure fuel pump (HPFP) seal failure (dumping fuel into oil, causing bearing damage). Rebuilt F154 units include upgraded wastegate hardware, revised HPFP seals, and new timing chains.
entries.cta: Get quotes for F154 CB Ferrari engine replacement

entries.title: F154 CD 3.9L Petrol
entries.description: Engine The F154 CD 3.9L V8 powers the Ferrari F8 Tributo and F8 Spider (2019–2023) with 720 hp, and the SF90 Stradale’s combustion engine component (with hybrid assist). Same failure modes as the F154 CB – turbo actuator wear and HPFP failure – but with higher mileage thresholds (typically 40,000+ miles before issues appear). Rebuilt units include the same upgraded components plus recalibrated ECU mapping for reliability.
entries.cta: Compare F154 CD Ferrari engine prices

entries.title: F140 FC 6.0L Petrol
entries.description: Engine The F140 FC 6.0L V12 powered the Ferrari 599 GTB Fiorano (2006–2012) with 620 hp, and the 599 GTO (2010–2012) with 670 hp. Catastrophic failures include crankshaft timing sprocket wear (teeth shear, causing chain slip and valve contact) and oil pump chain stretch at sustained high RPM. Rebuilt F140 units include a hardened steel crankshaft sprocket, revised oil pump drive chain, and new timing components throughout.
entries.cta: Get quotes for F140 FC Ferrari 599 engine replacement

entries.title: F140 GA 6.5L Petrol
entries.description: Engine The F140 GA 6.5L V12 powers the Ferrari 812 Superfast and 812 GTS (2017–present) with 800 hp, and the 812 Competizione with 830 hp. Primary failure point is oil pump chain stretch and timing chain wear beyond 60,000 miles – though newer metallurgy makes this less common than earlier F140 variants. Rebuilt 812 engines include upgraded chain tensioners, revised oil pump components, and factory-spec cylinder head reconditioning.
entries.cta: Compare F140 GA Ferrari 812 engine prices

entries.title: F140 EB 6.3L Petrol
entries.description: Engine The F140 EB 6.3L V12 powered the Ferrari FF (2011–2016, F149 chassis) with 660 hp. Known for timing chain stretch and camshaft phaser failure, particularly in cars used for short journeys. Rebuilt units include upgraded phaser assemblies, new timing chains, and revised oiling passages for the VVT system.
entries.cta: Get quotes for F140 EB Ferrari FF engine replacement

entries.title: F140 FC/FE 6.3L Petrol
entries.description: Hybrid The F140 FC 6.3L V12 (with F140 FE hybrid designation) powered the Ferrari LaFerrari (2013–2015) – 800 hp from the V12 plus 163 hp from the HY-KERS electric motor. Hybrid system complexity means replacement is specialist-only; common failures include MGU-K bearing wear and battery degradation alongside combustion engine issues. Rebuilt units include a fully reconditioned V12 plus hybrid system certification from Ferrari-trained technicians.
entries.cta: Enquire about F140 FC LaFerrari hybrid engine availability

entries.title: F163 DE 3.0L Petrol
entries.description: Hybrid Engine The F163 DE 3.0L twin-turbo V6 hybrid powers the Ferrari 296 GTB and 296 GTS (2021–present) producing 663 hp from petrol plus 167 hp electric (830 hp total). Early replacement drivers include turbo oil supply line fitting leaks and intercooler hose delamination. Rebuilt hybrid units require HV (high-voltage) system certification and include upgraded turbo oil feed lines, replacement intercooler hoses, and full hybrid battery/inverter health verification.
entries.cta: Get quotes for F163 DE Ferrari 296 hybrid engine replacement

entries.title: F131 B 3.6L Petrol
entries.description: Engine The F131 B 3.6L V8 powered the Ferrari 360 Modena and 360 Spider (1999–2004, Tipo F131 chassis) with 400 hp. Most common failure is cylinder liner wear (chrome-plated liners degrade, causing oil consumption and loss of compression) and timing belt tensioner failure. Rebuilt 360 engines include relined cylinders with Nikasil coating, new timing belts and tensioners, and full valve train reconditioning.
entries.cta: Compare F131 B Ferrari 360 engine prices

entries.title: F131 GT 3.6L Petrol
entries.description: Engine The F131 GT variant powered the Ferrari 360 Challenge Stradale (2003–2004) with 425 hp, plus track-focused modifications. Same failure modes as the standard F131 B – liner wear and belt tensioner issues – but with higher performance head components. Rebuilt Challenge Stradale engines include upgraded valve springs, Nikasil liners, and competition-grade timing components.
entries.cta: Get quotes for F131 GT Ferrari 360 Challenge Stradale engine replacement

engineCodeDirectory.closing: Can't find your engine code listed above? Use our full Ferrari engine code directory below — every code from the last 75 years — or enter your registration number for an instant match to the correct replacement engine with warranty.

directory.h3: Full Ferrari Engine Code Directory (All Years)
directory.label: View all Ferrari engine codes (45+ codes, 1950–2025)
directory.intro: A comprehensive alphabetical listing of every Ferrari engine code from the last 75 years. Use the search above or enter your registration for an instant match to your specific engine. All codes link to a quote request.

engineYears.h2: Ferrari Engines by Year – What Was Fitted & When
engineYears.intro: This section maps every major Ferrari engine family across the full modern production timeline - from the precision-engineered flat-12 and naturally aspirated V8s of the 1990s through to the F136 and F154 turbocharged eras, the landmark 6.5-litre F140 naturally aspirated V12, and the hybrid F163 V6 era - so owners, collectors, and specialist workshops can identify the exact engine fitted by model year and source precision-rebuilt replacement or overhauled units from UK Ferrari specialists.

years.preview: F355 V8 and 456/550 V12: The High-Watermark Naturally Aspirated Era Performance Landmark
years.description: The mid-to-late 1990s represent Ferrari at its naturally aspirated peak - the screaming 3.5-litre Tipo F129 V8 in the F355 and the 5.5-litre Tipo F116 V12 in the 456 and 550 Maranello established the benchmark for analogue Ferrari character that owners still reference today, while also introducing some of the brand's most notorious ownership challenges.
keyChanges: Tipo F129 3.5-litre V8 debuts in F355 (1994) - flat-plane crank, 8,250 rpm redline, 375 bhp; a fundamental advance over the 348's architecture
456 GT launches with Tipo F116 5.5-litre naturally aspirated V12 - 442 bhp, front-engine layout, the first modern Ferrari grand tourer with a proper rear seat
550 Maranello launches 1996 with evolved Tipo F133 5.5-litre V12 - 485 bhp, front-engined two-seater replacing the mid-engine Testarossa bloodline
F355 introduces titanium connecting rods and five-valve-per-cylinder architecture - highest specific output of any Ferrari road engine to this point
F1 electro-hydraulic gearbox offered as option on F355 from 1997 - the first paddle-shift system on a Ferrari road car
mainEngines: Ferrari Tipo F129 - 3.5-litre naturally aspirated flat-plane V8, 375 bhp at 8,250 rpm (Ferrari F355, F355 Spider, F355 GTS, F355 Berlinetta)
Ferrari Tipo F116 - 5.5-litre naturally aspirated V12, 442 bhp at 6,250 rpm (Ferrari 456 GT, 456 GTA, 456M)
Ferrari Tipo F133 - 5.5-litre naturally aspirated V12, 485 bhp at 7,000 rpm (Ferrari 550 Maranello)
knownFor: F355 Tipo F129 exhaust manifold (header) failure is a near-universal event - Ferrari specialists regard header cracking as essentially inevitable; cracked headers require engine-out removal and full rebuild at UK specialist costs of £15,000–£25,000+

Five-valve bronze valve guides in the F129 are a documented critical weakness - guide failure causes compression loss and smoke; valve guide replacement requires full engine-out work and represents the most expensive F355 maintenance event

Timing belt replacement on the F355 requires full engine removal - a 40–60 hour specialist job; this service must be performed every five years or 30,000 miles regardless of condition and costs £4,000–£8,000 at UK authorised specialists

550 Maranello Tipo F133 is regarded as one of the most reliable modern Ferrari engines - the few documented concerns are coolant crossover pipe O-ring failure and rear main seal weeping at high mileage, both addressable without engine removal
years.ticker: Common Replacement Enquiries: Ferrari F355 engine rebuild UK - Ferrari F355 engine replacement - Ferrari 456 V12 engine for sale UK - Ferrari 550 Maranello engine rebuild - Ferrari F355 valve guide repair UK
years.cta: Get specialist quotes for 1994–1999 Ferrari F355 and 550 Maranello precision engine rebuilds from UK Ferrari specialists

years.preview: 360 Modena and 575M: Aluminium Spaceframe and V8 Evolution Major Engine Family Introduction
years.description: The Ferrari 360 Modena of 1999 introduced the brand's first all-aluminium spaceframe chassis alongside a comprehensively redesigned 3.6-litre naturally aspirated V8 - the Tipo F131 - addressing many of the F355's notorious service requirements while raising performance and refining the ownership proposition, while the 575M continued the 550's V12 bloodline at 5.7 litres.
keyChanges: Tipo F131 3.6-litre naturally aspirated V8 debuts in Ferrari 360 Modena - flat-plane crank, 400 bhp at 8,500 rpm; eliminates the five-valve-per-cylinder complexity of F129
Aluminium chassis spaceframe replaces steel; total dry weight reduced to 1,390 kg - 40 kg lighter than F355
Timing belts eliminated on V8 - Tipo F131 uses timing chains for the first time in a Ferrari V8; major ownership benefit
575M Maranello launches 2002 with Tipo F133E 5.7-litre - 515 bhp; F1 gearbox standard; HGTE performance pack available
Euro 3 compliance across both engine families - closed-loop catalysts and revised injection management
mainEngines: Ferrari Tipo F131 - 3.6-litre naturally aspirated V8, 400 bhp at 8,500 rpm (Ferrari 360 Modena, 360 Spider, 360 Challenge Stradale)
Ferrari Tipo F133E - 5.7-litre naturally aspirated V12, 515 bhp at 7,250 rpm (Ferrari 575M Maranello, 575M Superamerica)
knownFor: Tipo F131 chain drive eliminates the most costly F355 ownership burden - 360 Modena engine maintenance is significantly more straightforward than its predecessor; the engine itself is structurally sound

F1 electro-hydraulic gearbox on 360 Modena and 575M is the primary ownership concern - pump seals, solenoids, and actuator wear are characteristic at 30,000+ miles; budget £3,000–£8,000 for specialist F1 gearbox service at UK authorised centres
360 Challenge Stradale Tipo F131 pushed to 425 bhp - additional mechanical stress raises valve spring and bearing wear rates; a used Stradale must have documented service history reviewed carefully
Tipo F133E 5.7-litre in 575M shows strong long-term durability - rear main seal oil seepage and cam cover gasket weeping are the most common age-related concerns; structural engine failures are rare
years.ticker: Common Replacement Enquiries: Ferrari 360 Modena engine rebuild UK - Ferrari 360 V8 engine for sale - Ferrari 575M engine replacement UK - Ferrari F131 engine UK
years.cta: Compare 1999–2004 Ferrari 360 and 575M engine prices from UK Ferrari specialists

years.preview: F430 and 612 Scaglietti: F136 V8 Debut and V12 Grand Touring Major Engine Family Introduction Performance Landmark
years.description: The Ferrari F430 of 2004 introduced the all-new Tipo F136 4.3-litre V8 - a clean-sheet architecture inspired by the Enzo's V12 internals, sharing a block with contemporary Maserati but with entirely new internals including a flat-plane crank and the Enzo's variable valve timing - establishing the engine family that would underpin Ferrari's V8 range for the following two decades.
keyChanges: Tipo F136 4.3-litre V8 debuts in Ferrari F430 - flat-plane crank from Enzo V12, variable valve timing, 483 bhp at 8,500 rpm
F136 block shared with Maserati GranTurismo platform; Ferrari internals entirely unique - not a Maserati engine
430 Scuderia launches 2007 with F136 ED uprated to 503 bhp - titanium con-rods, race-derived ECU calibration
Ferrari 612 Scaglietti launches 2004 with Tipo F133G 5.7-litre V12, 540 bhp - four-seat grand touring flagship
F430 introduces E-Diff electronic differential and F1-SuperFast II paddle gearbox - the most technologically advanced Ferrari road car to this point
Euro 4 compliance from 2006 via revised injection management and catalyst updates on both engine families
mainEngines: Ferrari Tipo F136 E - 4.3-litre naturally aspirated V8, 483 bhp at 8,500 rpm (Ferrari F430, F430 Spider, F430 Scuderia)
Ferrari Tipo F136 ED - 4.3-litre naturally aspirated V8 uprated, 503 bhp at 8,500 rpm (Ferrari 430 Scuderia, 430 Scuderia Spider 16M)
Ferrari Tipo F133G - 5.7-litre naturally aspirated V12, 540 bhp at 7,250 rpm (Ferrari 612 Scaglietti)
knownFor: Tipo F136 is regarded by Ferrari specialists as one of the most mechanically sound V8 architectures in the brand's history - chain-driven, structurally robust, and with no documented catastrophic failure modes in standard road use

F1-SuperFast II paddle gearbox selector fork wear is the most documented ownership issue - gear engagement hesitancy and rough changes from 40,000 miles; a specialist gearbox service at UK Ferrari centres runs £4,000–£7,000

430 Scuderia F136 ED titanium internals require precision-rebuild expertise; standard workshop tooling is insufficient - only authorised Ferrari Classiche or specialist rebuilders should open these engines

Tipo F133G 612 V12 demonstrates excellent structural longevity but camshaft sensor failures and throttle body fouling are common MIL triggers above 50,000 miles; disproportionately affected by infrequent use
years.ticker: Common Replacement Enquiries: Ferrari F430 engine rebuild UK - Ferrari F136 engine for sale UK - Ferrari 430 Scuderia engine replacement - Ferrari 612 V12 engine UK - Ferrari F430 V8 engine UK
years.cta: Find 2004–2009 Ferrari F430 F136 V8 and 612 V12 engine prices from UK Ferrari specialists

years.preview: 458 Italia, California and FF: F136 Evolution at Its Peak Performance Landmark
years.description: The Ferrari 458 Italia of 2009 represented the absolute zenith of the naturally aspirated F136 V8 family - uprated to 4.5 litres in the F136 FB specification, producing 562 bhp at a 9,000 rpm redline with direct injection, achieving levels of naturally aspirated V8 performance that Ferrari has since acknowledged will not be exceeded by a combustion-only V8 in their production range.
keyChanges: Tipo F136 FB 4.5-litre naturally aspirated V8 debuts in Ferrari 458 Italia - first direct injection Ferrari mid-engine V8; 562 bhp at 9,000 rpm
F136 IB 4.3-litre introduced in California (2009) - front-engine layout with wet-sump lubrication; first Ferrari with a retractable hardtop
458 Speciale launches 2013 with F136 FL uprated to 597 bhp - highest output naturally aspirated road car in Ferrari history at the time
Ferrari FF (2011) introduces four-wheel drive for the first time in Ferrari production history; F140 EB 6.3-litre V12 with 660 bhp
Ferrari F12berlinetta (2012) uses F140 FD 6.3-litre V12 - 730 bhp at 8,250 rpm; the most powerful naturally aspirated road car in the world at launch
Euro 5 compliance across all new engine codes from 2011
mainEngines: Ferrari Tipo F136 FB - 4.5-litre naturally aspirated V8, 562 bhp at 9,000 rpm (Ferrari 458 Italia, 458 Spider)
Ferrari Tipo F136 FL - 4.5-litre naturally aspirated V8, 597 bhp at 9,000 rpm (Ferrari 458 Speciale, 458 Speciale A)
Ferrari Tipo F136 IB / IH - 4.3-litre naturally aspirated V8, 453–483 bhp (Ferrari California, California 30)
Ferrari Tipo F140 EB - 6.3-litre naturally aspirated V12, 660 bhp at 8,000 rpm (Ferrari FF)
Ferrari Tipo F140 FD - 6.3-litre naturally aspirated V12, 730 bhp at 8,250 rpm (Ferrari F12berlinetta)
knownFor: 458 F136 FB is consistently rated as one of the finest road car engines ever produced - no documented structural failure modes; camshaft damage at 60,000 miles is a rare but documented failure on a small number of UK cars; Ferrari UK authorised warranty repair in documented cases

458 Italia's direct injection system introduces intake valve carbon buildup as a potential long-term maintenance item - walnut blasting at 80,000-mile intervals is recommended by UK Ferrari specialists

California F136 IB wet-sump architecture is a point of differentiation from the mid-engine dry-sump 458; throttle body fouling causes rough idle on low-mileage stored cars - a common trait of infrequently used front-engine Ferraris

F12berlinetta F140 FD 730 bhp is structurally exceptional at standard output - battery drain from the complex electrical systems is the most frequently reported fault on stored or low-mileage examples; the engine itself is essentially beyond reproach
years.ticker: Common Replacement Enquiries: Ferrari 458 engine rebuild UK - Ferrari F136 4.5 engine for sale UK - Ferrari 458 Speciale engine UK - Ferrari F12 V12 engine replacement - Ferrari California engine rebuild UK
years.cta: Get 2009–2015 Ferrari 458 and F12berlinetta engine rebuild quotes from UK Ferrari specialists

years.preview: LaFerrari: HY-KERS Hybrid Hypercar Major Engine Family Introduction Technology Introduction Performance Landmark
years.description: The 2013 LaFerrari introduced Ferrari's first series-production hybrid system - the HY-KERS unit combining a bespoke F140 FE 6.3-litre naturally aspirated V12 with a 120 kW electric motor for a combined 963 PS output, representing Ferrari's most significant powertrain landmark since the introduction of the V12 itself, and the direct technical precursor to all subsequent Ferrari hybrid systems.
keyChanges: F140 FE 6.3-litre naturally aspirated V12 produces 800 PS at 9,000 rpm - derived from F12berlinetta's F140 FD but with revised cams and higher compression
HY-KERS (Kinetic Energy Recovery System) adds 120 kW / 163 PS electric motor - F1-derived technology, provides kinetic energy recovery under braking
Combined output: 963 PS - 0–62 mph in under 3.0 seconds; the fastest accelerating series-production Ferrari at launch
7.4 kWh lithium-ion battery pack - no external charging; charged exclusively via regenerative braking
Only 499 coupes and 210 Aperta convertibles produced globally; among the most valued modern Ferraris in UK collections
F140 FE uses dry-sump lubrication and titanium connecting rods - precision engineering at every tolerance
mainEngines: Ferrari Tipo F140 FE - 6.3-litre naturally aspirated V12, 800 PS at 9,000 rpm + 120 kW HY-KERS eMotor = 963 PS combined (Ferrari LaFerrari, LaFerrari Aperta)
knownFor: HY-KERS system was the world's first road car KERS system - F1-derived regenerative braking technology integrated flawlessly at road car reliability levels; battery pack health on a decade-old LaFerrari is the primary long-term technical monitoring point

F140 FE V12 is regarded by Ferrari's own engineers as the most advanced naturally aspirated production engine the brand has ever built - titanium internals, 94 kW per litre specific output, and near-zero mechanical failure rate in road use

LaFerrari service requires Ferrari Classiche authorised-specialist involvement only - no independent workshop should approach this car's powertrain; specialist service in the UK costs £12,000–£20,000 per major service cycle
HY-KERS battery pack integrity check should be performed every three years on stored examples - lithium-ion cell dormancy is a concern for collection cars not regularly exercised
years.cta: Contact a UK Ferrari Classiche specialist for LaFerrari powertrain service and engineering enquiries

years.preview: 488 GTB, Portofino and GTC4Lusso: F154 Twin-Turbo Era Major Engine Family Introduction Technology Introduction
years.description: The Ferrari 488 GTB of 2015 marked Ferrari's most controversial powertrain decision in a generation - replacing the beloved naturally aspirated F136 V8 with the F154 3.9-litre twin-turbocharged V8, responding to Euro 6 emissions demands while delivering 661 bhp and winning International Engine of the Year five consecutive years from 2016 to 2020.
keyChanges: Tipo F154 CB 3.9-litre twin-turbo V8 debuts in Ferrari 488 GTB - replaces F136 family; 661 bhp at 8,000 rpm, 561 lb ft from 3,000 rpm; Euro 6 compliant
Hot-V twin-turbo layout with direct injection - turbochargers positioned inside the V for shorter spool time; 0–62 mph in 3.0 seconds
Ferrari California T (2014, pre-488) first road car to receive F154 precursor - California T uses front-engine F154 variant from 2014 onwards
488 Pista (2018) raises F154 output to 711 bhp at 8,000 rpm - highest power-to-weight ratio of any Ferrari road car bar hypercars at launch
GTC4Lusso (2016) uses updated F140 FC 6.3-litre V12, 681 bhp - successor to FF; all-wheel drive, four seats
GTC4Lusso T uses F154 CA front-engine V8 variant - first front-engined V8 coupe in Ferrari history
mainEngines: Ferrari Tipo F154 CB - 3.9-litre twin-turbo V8, 661 bhp at 8,000 rpm (Ferrari 488 GTB, 488 Spider)
Ferrari Tipo F154 EA - 3.9-litre twin-turbo V8, 711 bhp at 8,000 rpm (Ferrari 488 Pista, 488 Pista Spider)
Ferrari Tipo F154 CA - 3.9-litre twin-turbo V8, 600 bhp (Ferrari GTC4Lusso T, Portofino)
Ferrari Tipo F140 FC - 6.3-litre naturally aspirated V12, 681 bhp at 8,250 rpm (Ferrari GTC4Lusso)
Ferrari Tipo F154 BB - 3.9-litre twin-turbo V8 (Ferrari Portofino M)
knownFor: F154 twin-turbo V8 won International Engine of the Year five consecutive times - the turbo architecture is widely accepted by the specialist community as technically superior to the F136 in power delivery, if different in acoustic character

Turbo actuator and wastegate calibration sensitivity is the primary ongoing maintenance point - wastegate rattle on cold start is a known acoustic characteristic, not a structural fault; some early 488 cars required ECU recalibration

Direct injection carbon buildup on intake valves should be treated as a 60,000-mile service item - the twin-turbo layout means intake valves never see fuel washing and deposits accumulate faster than on port-injected predecessors

488 Pista F154 EA at 711 bhp places higher thermal stress on turbocharger bearings - bearing inspection is recommended at every major service interval (three years / 20,000 miles at UK specialist rates of £4,000–£6,000)
years.ticker: Common Replacement Enquiries: Ferrari 488 engine rebuild UK - Ferrari F154 V8 engine for sale UK - Ferrari 488 Pista engine UK - Ferrari Portofino engine replacement UK - Ferrari GTC4Lusso V12 engine UK
years.cta: Get 2015–2020 Ferrari 488 and Portofino F154 twin-turbo engine rebuild quotes from UK specialists

years.preview: 812 Superfast/GTS and the F140 GA 6.5-litre V12: The Last Naturally Aspirated Ferrari V12 Performance Landmark
years.description: The Ferrari 812 Superfast of 2017 introduced the F140 GA 6.5-litre naturally aspirated V12 - the largest, highest-revving, and most powerful naturally aspirated engine Ferrari has ever fitted to a series-production road car, and widely regarded as the definitive expression of the front-engine V12 grand touring bloodline that began with the 365 GTB/4 Daytona fifty years earlier.
keyChanges: Tipo F140 GA 6.5-litre naturally aspirated V12 debuts in 812 Superfast - enlarged from F12's 6.3-litre; 789 bhp at 8,500 rpm, 530 lb ft at 7,000 rpm
Rear-wheel steering introduced on 812 - the first Ferrari road car with four-wheel steering; combined with electronic rear-wheel torque vectoring
812 GTS (2020) is the first open-top V12 front-engine Ferrari - introduces F140 GA in convertible configuration
812 Competizione (2021) pushes F140 GA to 830 bhp at 9,500 rpm - highest-revving naturally aspirated production road car engine in the world at launch
Euro 6d compliance maintained throughout - achieved via revised secondary air management and updated catalyst calibration without turbocharging
Ferrari explicitly confirms the F140 GA is the final naturally aspirated V12 in their production road car range
mainEngines: Ferrari Tipo F140 GA - 6.5-litre naturally aspirated V12, 789 bhp at 8,500 rpm (Ferrari 812 Superfast, 812 GTS)
Ferrari Tipo F140 HC - 6.5-litre naturally aspirated V12, 830 bhp at 9,500 rpm (Ferrari 812 Competizione, 812 Competizione A)
knownFor: Tipo F140 GA is widely considered the greatest naturally aspirated production car engine ever made - 789 bhp without forced induction at 8,500 rpm, with zero documented structural failure modes in standard road use; reliability rated exceptional across the owner community
Battery drain from the 812's complex 12V electrical architecture is the most commonly reported ownership concern - a battery tender is mandatory for any car that will stand for more than two weeks
Throttle body fouling on low-mileage examples causes idle roughness and MIL warnings - a direct consequence of infrequent use; resolution is specialist throttle body cleaning, not engine work

812 Competizione F140 HC at 9,500 rpm represents the absolute limit of naturally aspirated reciprocating engine technology - specialist service is mandatory; no independent workshop should attempt internal engine work on these units
years.ticker: Common Replacement Enquiries: Ferrari 812 Superfast engine UK - Ferrari 812 V12 engine rebuild - Ferrari 812 GTS engine for sale UK - Ferrari F140 engine specialist UK - Ferrari 6.5 V12 engine service UK
years.cta: Source specialist-rebuild Ferrari 812 V12 engine services and parts from UK Ferrari Classiche specialists

years.preview: SF90 Stradale, 296 GTB and Roma: The Hybrid and F154/F163 Era Major Engine Family Introduction Technology Introduction
years.description: The 2019 SF90 Stradale introduced Ferrari's first series-production plug-in hybrid system - a 4.0-litre F154 FA twin-turbo V8 producing 780 PS combined with three electric motors for 986 PS total - marking the most significant Ferrari powertrain shift since the first V12, while the 2021 296 GTB introduced an entirely new F163 3.0-litre twin-turbo V6 as Ferrari's smallest modern road car engine, paired with a 122 kW electric motor.
keyChanges: SF90 Stradale (2019) debut: F154 FA 4.0-litre twin-turbo V8 + three electric motors + 7.9 kWh battery = 986 PS combined; Ferrari's first series-production PHEV and first all-wheel drive mid-engine Ferrari
Ferrari Roma (2020) introduces updated F154 CK 3.9-litre twin-turbo V8 in front-engine 2+2 configuration - 620 bhp; 8-speed dual-clutch standard
Ferrari Purosangue (2022) debuts the F140 HD naturally aspirated V12 in SUV configuration - 725 bhp at 7,750 rpm; Ferrari's first four-door, four-seat production car
Ferrari 296 GTB (2021) introduces F163 BC 3.0-litre twin-turbo 120° V6 + 122 kW eMotor = 818 bhp combined; Ferrari's first V6 road car engine since the Dino 246 in 1974
Euro 6d Final compliance across all new models from 2021
SF90 XX Stradale (2023) pushes F154 FA system to 1,030 PS - highest output series-production Ferrari in history
mainEngines: Ferrari Tipo F154 FA - 4.0-litre twin-turbo V8, 780 PS at 7,500 rpm + 3x eMotors 220 PS = 986–1,030 PS (Ferrari SF90 Stradale, SF90 Spider, SF90 XX Stradale)
Ferrari Tipo F154 CK - 3.9-litre twin-turbo V8, 620 bhp at 7,500 rpm (Ferrari Roma, Roma Spider)
Ferrari Tipo F163 BC - 3.0-litre twin-turbo 120° V6, 654 bhp at 8,000 rpm + 122 kW eMotor = 818 bhp (Ferrari 296 GTB, 296 GTS)
Ferrari Tipo F140 HD - 6.5-litre naturally aspirated V12, 725 bhp at 7,750 rpm (Ferrari Purosangue)
knownFor: SF90 F154 FA PHEV system is extraordinarily capable but the level of electronic sophistication means all powertrain work must remain within the Ferrari authorised specialist network - hybrid battery pack service is a £10,000–£20,000 exercise at UK Ferrari centres

F163 V6 in the 296 GTB is Ferrari's boldest technical statement of the decade - the 120° bank angle is derived directly from their F1 power unit architecture; early ownership data from the enthusiast community suggests the engine is impeccably refined and reliable

Roma F154 CK is the most accessible modern Ferrari powertrain from a servicing perspective - front-engine layout with conventional access points makes independent specialist work viable compared to mid-engine applications

Purosangue F140 HD V12 carries the naturally aspirated V12 bloodline into Ferrari's first SUV - long-term drivetrain durability data is building positively across the global owner community as the first three-year service cycles complete

faq.h2: Ferrari Engine Replacement – Frequently Asked Questions
faq.intro: ** Quick, honest answers to the most common questions Ferrari owners ask before comparing engine replacement prices and finding a UK specialist.

items.question: How much does a Ferrari engine replacement cost in the UK?
items.answer: A Ferrari engine replacement typically costs between £8,500 and £22,000 for a reconditioned or rebuilt unit, depending on the model. A used Ferrari 360 F131 V8 might start around £6,000–£8,000 supply‑only, while a rebuilt F430 F136E with upgraded bearings ranges from £11,500–£14,500 supply‑and‑fit. A fully rebuilt F140 V12 for an F12 or 812 can exceed £18,000–£22,000. Main dealer prices are often 40–60% higher. Always confirm whether fitting, delivery, and VAT are included in the quoted price.
items.cta: Compare Ferrari engine replacement prices now

items.question: What affects the cost of a Ferrari engine replacement?
items.answer: Four main factors drive Ferrari engine replacement costs: engine size and family (F131/F136 V8 is less expensive than F140 V12), condition (rebuilt with upgrades costs more than used standard), vehicle age and complexity (a 458 requires more labour than a 360), and whether you choose supply‑only or supply‑and‑fit – fitting typically adds £1,000–£2,500 depending on workshop location (London rates can be 20–30% higher). Our comparison tool brings quotes from multiple UK Ferrari specialists so you can see exactly what’s included.
items.cta: Get quotes from UK Ferrari engine specialists

items.question: Is it worth replacing the engine in my Ferrari?
items.answer: In most cases, yes – especially if the chassis, gearbox, and bodywork are in good condition. A rebuilt Ferrari engine with upgraded components (e.g., upgraded oil pump drive gear on F136/F140, revised timing chain tensioners on F154) often outlasts the original unit. With Ferrari values holding strong – a 360 Modena now worth £35,000–£55,000 – spending £9,500–£12,500 on a rebuilt F131 V8 makes far more financial sense than selling the car as a non‑runner or buying a replacement vehicle. Our marketplace lets you compare rebuilt versus used options for your specific model.
items.cta: Compare Ferrari engine supply and fit deals

items.question: What's included in a Ferrari engine supply and fit service?
items.answer: A full Ferrari engine supply‑and‑fit service includes: sourcing the correct engine code (e.g., F136E, F140FC, F154CB), removal of your old unit, installation of the replacement engine, new fluids, filters, belts (where applicable), and a minimum 12‑month unlimited mileage warranty. Most specialists also include an initial test‑drive, diagnostics check, and responsible disposal of your old engine. Fitting costs typically range from £1,100–£2,500 depending on model complexity. Some suppliers also offer nationwide vehicle collection and delivery. Always ask for a written breakdown of what’s covered.
items.cta: Find reconditioned Ferrari engines with comprehensive warranty cover

items.question: How long does a Ferrari engine replacement take?
items.answer: A like‑for‑like Ferrari engine swap on common models such as a 360, F430, or 458 typically takes 3–5 working days. More complex models – an FF, GTC4Lusso, or F12 – can take 5–7 days due to additional systems integration (four‑wheel drive, electric power steering, or hybrid components). The biggest variable is engine availability: our network of UK Ferrari specialists keeps commonly replaced engine codes (F131, F136E, F154CB) in stock, but rarer units (F140EB, F163BC) may add 5–10 days for sourcing. Ask each supplier for their current lead time when requesting quotes.
items.cta: Use our free comparison tool to see lead times for your Ferrari

items.question: Do reconditioned Ferrari engines come with warranty?
items.answer: Yes – every reconditioned or rebuilt Ferrari engine supplied through EngineMarket carries a minimum 12‑month unlimited mileage warranty as standard. Many UK specialists also offer extended 24‑month coverage on fully remanufactured units (typically at an additional £500–£1,000). The standard warranty covers major internal components: crankshaft, connecting rods, pistons, timing chain, oil pump, bearings, and gaskets. Labour for warranty repairs is usually included, but always confirm exact terms – including whether the warranty is parts‑only or parts‑and‑labour – directly with your chosen supplier before proceeding.
items.cta: Compare Ferrari engine warranty options from UK specialists

items.question: Should I choose a used or reconditioned Ferrari engine?
items.answer: A used Ferrari engine is cheaper upfront (typically £6,000–£11,000) and may be suitable if you’re selling the car soon or working with a very tight budget – but it carries the risk of the same original failure recurring (e.g., F136 crankshaft bearings, F154 timing chain tensioner). A reconditioned or rebuilt engine costs more (typically £9,500–£17,000) but includes new or upgraded components, full bench testing, and a 12–24 month warranty, offering another 100,000+ miles of reliable service. For Ferrari models with known weak points – F430 (crank walk), 458 (oil pump shaft), 488 (timing chain) – we strongly recommend reconditioned with upgraded parts.
items.cta: Get quotes for reconditioned vs used Ferrari engines

items.question: Where can I find Ferrari engine specialists near me?
items.answer: EngineMarket connects you with a UK‑wide network of independent Ferrari engine specialists who stock specific engine codes and offer supply‑and‑fit. While Ferrari expertise is concentrated around major cities (London, Birmingham, Manchester, Leeds, Bristol) and areas with strong performance car scenes (Buckinghamshire, Surrey, Cheshire), our network covers all UK regions. Simply enter your registration number – our tool instantly identifies your Ferrari model and engine code, then matches you with nearby pre‑vetted specialists who can supply the exact unit. Nationwide delivery and vehicle collection are also available if there are no specialists in your immediate area.
items.cta: Find a Ferrari engine specialist near you

items.question: What are the most common Ferrari engine failures?
items.answer: The most common Ferrari engine failures that lead to replacement are: on the F136 V8 (F430, 458) – crankshaft thrust bearing wear (“crank walk”) on pre‑2007 cars and oil pump drive shaft shear on high‑RPM 458s; on the F154 twin‑turbo V8 (488, Portofino, Roma) – timing chain tensioner fatigue causing chain slack and potential valve timing errors; on the F131 V8 (360 Modena) – cylinder liner scoring leading to oil consumption; and on the F140 V12 (599, F12, 812) – oil pump drive gear slip causing sudden oil pressure loss. Early warning signs are rare, and these failures often turn catastrophic, making replacement the most common outcome.
items.cta: Check your Ferrari engine for these failure symptoms

items.question: How can I get the best price on a Ferrari engine?
items.answer: Compare multiple quotes – prices for the same Ferrari engine code can vary by £1,500–£2,500 between suppliers. For tight budgets, a used engine from a lower‑mileage donor car offers the lowest entry price. For long‑term ownership, a reconditioned engine with upgraded components (e.g., F136 with revised bearings, F154 with upgraded tensioners) costs more upfront but saves money over time. Consider supply‑only if you have a trusted garage that can fit the engine for less than a specialist’s fitting charge. Always ask three questions: Is VAT included? Is delivery included? Is the warranty parts‑and‑labour or parts‑only? Our free comparison tool does the legwork for you.
items.cta: Compare Ferrari engine prices from multiple UK specialists
